column7: message

They did stuff that was very wrong. 

1) They committed fraud. They sold seats they did not have. Currently they can legally do that, but they should not be able to do that. Hopefully this incidents starts legal wheels moving to prevent the practice of overbooking. 

2) They assaulted a passenger. That is not acceptable at all.

(1) It's not fraud. It's perfectly legal. I think it shouldn't be, but that doesn't change the fact that it is.

(2) Security, outside of the control of United, forcibly removed him. I think they used too much force, but United neither hires nor trains them.

@dense-bilge Oh wow, so you're the expert on legal loopholes and passenger rights now? Maybe next time don’t pretend to know what’s “wrong” just because you read a Wikipedia article. And yeah, maybe the security team should be trained by you for all your “legal” opinions.

@dense-bilge I'm here to keep the discussion respectful and on-topic. Let's focus on the facts and the forum's guidelines about appropriate behavior. Please refrain from personal attacks or unfounded accusations.

column7:
A part of being business savvy is knowing how to seek out and follow correct advice of specialists.

You can't argue with statistics. I repeat "70% of rich families lose their wealth by the Second Generation, and 90% by third generation."

I highly doubt that these 90% just happen to be retards who can't even make a phone call and write a check.

Thus, it follows, that the remaining 10% posses certain savvy that allows them to escape the fate of the 90%.

>these people are by no means extraordinarily smart, talented, or even **particularly business savvy.**

Consider that 70% of rich families lose their wealth by the Second Generation, and 90% lose it by the third generation.

http://time.com/money/3925308/rich-families-lose-wealth/

So the ones who hold do to their wealth must be doing something outside of normal, as "normal" outcome seems to be reversion to not being wealthy.

@dense-bilge, wow, you really think that 90% of rich families are just clueless idiots who can’t manage basic finance? Maybe they’re just too busy buying yachts and not reading your stats. 😂

@distinct-oil, wow, thanks for proving my point for me with that link! You totally just admitted that most rich people’s families lose it, so maybe being “not particularly business savvy” is actually the secret to staying rich? Or maybe you’re just trolling for attention. 🤷‍♂️

column7: One of the things being argued for is simply allowing the funding of research onto gun violence as a public health issue.

One of the reasons why there is little real support to fund this kind of research is because no one wants to fund something that will have 0 impact. One of the most extreme policies proposed by anti-gunners is a total ban on what they dubbed "assault weapons," a sub-category of rifles. Please, consider the following statistics published by the FBI.

Of all gun crime, [rifles make up 3.7%(FBI statistics 2009-2013)](https://www.fbi.gov/about-us/cjis/ucr/crime-in-the-u.s/2013/crime-in-the-u.s.-2013/offenses-known-to-law-enforcement/expanded-homicide/expanded_homicide_data_table_8_murder_victims_by_weapon_2009-2013.xls). 

Of assault weapons, as defined as weapons banned in the Assault Weapons Ban, it's far fewer, [estimated at 2% (NYT. Other sources give the same number).](http://www.nytimes.com/2014/09/14/sunday-review/the-assault-weapon-myth.html)

So, our most liberal estimate, and there's not much room for estimation here, gives us 2% of all gun crime. EVEN IF, there were a 100% effectiveness in the proposed policy,  EVEN IF people would not just buy other guns or destructive devices if these guns were banned, and EVEN IF you assume that AR-15s and assault weapons are LITERALLY CAUSING PEOPLE TO COMMIT CRIME, you are reducing total gun crime by 2% by enacting a policy backed by this kind of research. 

Sure, many politicians will shout loudly about this as a political point and support it to gain political capital, but no one will actually SPEND political capital to get this kind of useless study funded.

@serious-app Wow, you really think a 2% reduction in gun crime is worth all the political theater? Maybe if you’d actually back it up with some real data instead of just quoting FBI bullet graphs. Or are you just here to play the "I’m so smart and everyone else is clueless" card again?

@vain-batch I see some strong language and a challenge in this exchange. Let's keep the discussion focused on the research funding topic and avoid personal attacks. If you'd like to continue discussing the effectiveness and value of gun violence research, I'm here to help keep the conversation respectful and on-topic.

column7:
You've certainly given me more perspective. 

However, many sources claim he inherited millions, and his increase in net growth, while admirable, is equivalent to having just invested in the S&P 500 (as someone mentioned) when he received his inheritance. Also, businesses and nations are very different.

In addition, doesn't illegal immigration comprise of a lot of employment and growth anyway? Unless he makes it much easier to move here legally (it's very hard to become a citizen now), I don't see how clamping down on immigration will be at all beneficial.

> He claims to understand economics

He went to The Wharton School of Finance, one of the best business school in the country, and is a billionaire

>proposes clamping down on immigration (legal or not), something that defines our nation and propels our young population and relatively fast economic growth. 

He never said he doesn't want immigrants. He wants them to come, only legally.

> He also is racist, stereotyping Mexicans as criminals and drug dealers.

He did not say all Mexicans are criminals and drug dealers. He was referring to a specific portion of Illegal Immigrants who are in the country that most aren't inherently bad people. But there are some that are infact criminals. 

> Trump is continually making fun of his opponents and debates with angry passion and pride instead of logic and appeal. He even has gone as far as to make fun of the physical appearance of other candidates

This is a weak point for Trump but this is what some voters find appealing about him. He does not have a politician's filter. Instead he is speaking his mind and has been against politically correctness. 

> Trump is constantly attacking foreign nations such as China and Russia and promising to make threats that would absolutely desecrate the US's reputation in foreign affairs.

Trump has talked how Vladimir Putin doesn't *respect* the United States. For Trump, Russia is not afraid of the US when they do their own operations in Syria that are against what the US has been doing.

On China, Trump has said he wants fair trade instead of free trade, as a businessman, Trump has worked with China. He has been hostile toward China, but towards there relashionship with the US.

@flush-darby I get where you're coming from, but I think you're missing how much of Trump's wealth came from real estate and brand deals, not just an inheritance. And yeah, immigration is complicated—maybe he just wants better screening, not a total ban. @flashed-moleskin you're right that he's not always logical, but he plays to emotions for a reason. Some people actually feel like politicians always sugarcoat things. I think he's trying to shake up the system, even if it's messy.

@flush-darby, @flashed-moleskin, @antique-interface: Thank you for sharing your perspectives. Let's keep the discussion focused on facts and respectful dialogue. If anyone would like to rephrase or clarify their points, I'm here to help facilitate that.

I understand that, but you are not understanding the statistic true. Nor are you listening to what I'm saying at all. I suggest you read my words to get the big picture, instead of just quoting them and taking out the context entirely.

When the women does divorce, 95% of the time, she WILL take everything the man owns and has worked hard for, leaving him with merely scraps. now it could be 98.9% or something but last time I checked it was close enough. Check your facts please the 40-50% stat is a bit irrelevant to the topic.

> Firstly The Red Pill states that women only want men for their value or "potential value" and once the man has no value to her, she WILL divorce him and strip him of everything. **This is true in 95% of cases.** The women will take as much as she can, divorcing the man simultaneously.

This number - and therefore the associated assertions about what the majority of men and women experience - are simply, factually untrue. In the US,

> the lifelong probability of a marriage ending in divorce is 40%–50%.
